notes.note: The unnamed "he" through this whole indictment is Pompey, whose conduct Cicero rehearses in a string of bitter pluperfects. The charge is that Pompey first built Caesar up politically, then lost his nerve and abandoned Italy without a plan.

notes.anchor: he had lost Picenum by his own fault
notes.note: Picenum, the Adriatic-coast region of central Italy and Pompey's own ancestral power-base, fell to Caesar's advance in late February 49 BC; its loss was felt as a particular humiliation for Pompey.

notes.anchor: letters from Domitius to him, his own to the consuls
notes.note: L. Domitius Ahenobarbus, holding Corfinium against Caesar, wrote begging Pompey to march to his relief while writing also to the consuls; Pompey refused and left him to his fate.
Brundisium (modern Brindisi), the great port on Italy's south-eastern heel, was Pompey's embarkation point for the crossing to Greece; his march there abandoned the inland Pompeian forces.

notes.anchor: Domitius, they say, on hearing the news, has given himself up
notes.note: When Pompey would not relieve him, Domitius's own troops at Corfinium forced his surrender to Caesar in late February 49 BC; Caesar spared him and his officers, the famous early act of clementia.
